distribution_analysis.most_covered_section: 4.1 Graphical Solutions of System of Linear Inequalities

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Steps to Solve a Linear Programming Problem
questions.question: If a linear programming problem has an optimal solution, where must that optimal solution occur?

options.A: At the center of the feasible region
options.B: At a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region
options.D: At the midpoint of the longest edge

questions.hint: The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Programming states that optimal solutions occur at vertices.
questions.explanation: Theorem 4.1 states that when an optimal value exists, it must occur at a corner point of the feasible region. Option A, C, and D are not generally true.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Steps to Solve a Linear Programming Problem
source_trace.content_anchor: optimal value must occur at a corner point

explanations_tiered.definition: The optimal solution always lies at a vertex of the feasible region if it exists.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Because the objective function is linear and the region is convex, extreme values occur at extreme points.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The best answer is always at a corner of the feasible shape.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Theorem 4.1 guarantees that if a maximum or minimum exists, at least one optimal solution will be a vertex.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This follows from the fact that a linear function on a convex polytope attains its maximum and minimum at extreme points.
reasoning_steps: Identify all vertices of feasible region
The best value among vertices is optimal
misconceptions: Thinking optimum could be at an interior point; interior points are never optimal for linear objectives unless the region is degenerate.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Optimum always at vertex (if it exists).

glossary_entry.definition: If an optimal solution exists, it occurs at a vertex.

flashcard.front: Where must an optimal solution occur in linear programming?
flashcard.back: At a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Where does optimal solution occur in LP?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: At a vertex of the feasible region.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Theorem 4.1 states optimum at corner point.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Steps to Solve a Linear Programming Problem
questions.question: If the feasible region is unbounded, what can be said about the existence of a maximum value?

options.C: A maximum may or may not exist
options.D: A maximum exists only at the origin

questions.hint: Unbounded regions can allow the objective to increase indefinitely, but constraints may still bound it.
questions.explanation: When the region is unbounded, a maximum may not exist if the objective can increase without bound, but it could exist if the objective is bounded above within the region. Option C captures this.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Steps to Solve a Linear Programming Problem
source_trace.content_anchor: if R is unbounded, a maximum may not exist

explanations_tiered.definition: An unbounded feasible region extends infinitely in some direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Whether a maximum exists depends on whether the objective function increases without bound in that direction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the region goes on forever, you might be able to make the objective larger and larger with no limit, so no maximum.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If the objective can increase infinitely along an unbounded direction, no maximum exists; if it decreases in that direction, a maximum may still exist at a vertex.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The existence of a maximum requires that the objective function be bounded above on the feasible set; unboundedness does not guarantee non-existence, but it is a necessary condition to check.
reasoning_steps: If objective increases in that direction → no maximum
If objective decreases in that direction → maximum may exist at a vertex
misconceptions: Assuming unbounded region always means no maximum.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Unbounded region: maximum may or may not exist.

glossary_entry.definition: Region that extends infinitely in at least one direction.

flashcard.front: If feasible region is unbounded, does maximum always exist?
flashcard.back: No; it may or may not exist depending on the objective.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does an unbounded feasible region guarantee no maximum?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No; maximum may still exist if objective is bounded above.

questions.glossary_definition: Region extending infinitely; may or may not yield a maximum.
questions.teacher_note: Provide examples: one with max, one without.
questions.learning_objective_tag: Analyze existence of optimum in unbounded regions
questions.solution_manual_note: Answer C. A maximum may or may not exist in an unbounded region.

subsection: Steps to Solve a Linear Programming Problem
question: If the feasible region is bounded, what can be guaranteed about the objective function \(Z = ax + by\)?

options.A: It has a maximum but no minimum
options.B: It has both a maximum and a minimum
options.C: It has a minimum but no maximum
options.D: It has neither a maximum nor a minimum

hint: A bounded feasible region is compact; a linear function attains both extremes.
explanation: Theorem 4.2 states that for a bounded feasible region, the objective function has both a maximum and a minimum value, each occurring at a vertex.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Steps to Solve a Linear Programming Problem
source_trace.content_anchor: if R is bounded, then Z has both a maximum and a minimum

explanations_tiered.definition: On a bounded feasible region, a linear objective function attains both a maximum and a minimum.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Because the region is closed and bounded (compact), a continuous function must have both extremes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the shape has edges all around, you'll find both a highest point and a lowest point.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Theorem 4.2 guarantees existence of both maximum and minimum on a bounded feasible region.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This follows from the Extreme Value Theorem applied to the linear objective over a compact convex set.
reasoning_steps: If yes, both max and min exist
Evaluate at vertices to find them
misconceptions: Thinking bounded region might have only max or only min.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Bounded region → both max and min exist.

glossary_entry.definition: Region that is finite in extent.

flashcard.front: If feasible region is bounded, what does linear programming guarantee?
flashcard.back: Both a maximum and a minimum exist.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does Theorem 4.2 guarantee for a bounded feasible region?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Both a maximum and a minimum exist.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Bounded region → max and min exist.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to extreme value theorem in calculus.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Both max and min exist for bounded region.

subject: Mathematics
subsection: Steps to Solve a Linear Programming Problem
question: Which condition must be checked to determine if a minimum exists when the feasible region is unbounded?

options.A: Whether the objective function can decrease without bound
options.B: Whether the origin is inside the region
options.D: Whether the region has at least one vertex

hint: If the objective can go to negative infinity (for minimization), no finite minimum exists.
explanation: For an unbounded region, a minimum exists only if the objective function is bounded below within the region. Option A correctly identifies the need to check if the objective can decrease indefinitely.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Steps to Solve a Linear Programming Problem
source_trace.content_anchor: if the open half plane determined by ax+by < m has no point in common with feasible region

explanations_tiered.definition: For a minimum to exist in an unbounded region, the objective must not be able to go to negative infinity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If decreasing the objective indefinitely is possible along an unbounded direction, no finite minimum exists.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you can keep making the value smaller forever, there's no lowest point.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Check if the objective function \(Z = ax + by\) has a lower bound on the feasible region; if it can approach \(-\infty\), min does not exist.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is equivalent to checking that the line \(ax+by = m\) can be lowered until it no longer touches the feasible region; if it can be lowered indefinitely, no minimum.
reasoning_steps: See if objective decreases in that direction
If no → minimum exists at a vertex
misconceptions: Assuming unbounded region always has a min if it has a max.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Unbounded region: check if objective can go to \(-\infty\).

glossary_entry.definition: Value below which objective cannot go.

flashcard.front: What must be checked for a minimum in an unbounded region?
flashcard.back: Whether the objective function can decrease without bound.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How to test if minimum exists in unbounded region?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Check if objective can decrease without bound; if yes, no finite minimum.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Test if objective is bounded below on feasible region.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine existence of optimum in unbounded regions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Unbounded decrease means no minimum.

subject: Mathematics
question: A special feed requires at least 30% protein. Corn has 10% protein, soybean meal has 60% protein. Which inequality represents the protein requirement?
hint: At least 30% means ≥ 0.3 of total weight.
explanation: The amount of protein \(0.1x+0.6y\) must be at least 30% of total mix \(x+y\), so \(0.1x+0.6y \ge 0.3(x+y)\). Option A represents at most 30%.

explanations_tiered.definition: A diet constraint ensures nutritional minimums are met.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The protein content from corn and soybean must be at least 30% of total weight.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The protein from both foods must be at least 30% of the total amount mixed.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since total feed is \(x+y\) kg, protein requirement is \(0.1x+0.6y \ge 0.3(x+y)\).

glossary_entry.definition: Ensures protein content meets minimum percentage.

flashcard.front: Corn (10% protein) and soybean (60% protein) are mixed. At least 30% protein required. Write inequality.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Write protein constraint for corn (10%) and soybean (60%) mixture requiring at least 30% protein.

subject: Mathematics
question: In a diet problem, the fiber requirement is at most 4% of total mix. Corn has 2% fiber, soybean has 6% fiber. Which constraint is correct?
explanation: The fiber content must be ≤ 4% of total mix, so \(0.02x+0.06y \le 0.04(x+y)\). Option B would be for at least 4%.

explanations_tiered.definition: A maximum fiber constraint limits fiber content to ≤ a percentage of total feed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Fiber from corn and soybean must be ≤ 4% of total weight.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The total fiber cannot exceed 4% of the whole mixture.

glossary_entry.definition: Limits fiber content to ≤ a specified percentage.

flashcard.front: Fiber at most 4%: corn 2%, soybean 6%. Write constraint.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Fiber at most 4%: corn 2%, soybean 6%. Write inequality.

subject: Mathematics
question: Corn costs Birr 30/kg and soybean costs Birr 90/kg. The daily feed must be at least 800 kg. What is the minimum cost objective function?
hint: Cost minimization uses objective \(Z = \text{cost}_1 \cdot x + \text{cost}_2 \cdot y\).
explanation: The cost is \(30x + 90y\), and we want to minimize it. Option A states the correct objective (without constraints shown, but constraints are separate). Option C incorrectly uses ≤ 800 instead of ≥ 800.

explanations_tiered.definition: The cost objective is the sum of cost per kg times kg of each ingredient.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Minimizing cost requires minimizing \(30x+90y\) while meeting nutritional constraints.
explanations_tiered.beginner: We want to spend as little as possible, so we minimize total cost.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Cost = 30x + 90y; we minimize Z because we want the cheapest mix.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The objective function is linear; minimizing it over the feasible region gives the least-cost diet.
reasoning_steps: Identify costs: 30 for corn, 90 for soybean
misconceptions: Maximizing cost instead of minimizing; using ≤ instead of ≥ for minimum feed amount.

glossary_entry.definition: Finding cheapest ingredient mix meeting requirements.

flashcard.front: Corn 30/kg, soybean 90/kg, need ≥800 kg daily. Write objective to minimize cost.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Cost minimization objective for corn (30/kg) and soybean (90/kg)?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize difference between max and min.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Minimize Z = 30x+90y is correct.

subject: Mathematics
question: Model A requires 9 labor hours for fabricating and 1 hour for finishing; Model B requires 12 and 3 hours respectively. Maximum fabricating hours are 180, finishing hours 30. Which constraint is correct?
explanation: Fabricating hours: \(9x+12y\) must be ≤ 180; finishing: \(x+3y\) must be ≤ 30. Option A is correct.

explanations_tiered.definition: Resource constraints limit total hours used to at most available hours.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each product consumes certain hours; total cannot exceed maximum.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You cannot use more than the hours available, so total hours ≤ limit.
explanations_tiered.advanced: These are the resource constraints that define part of the feasible region for the manufacturing problem.
reasoning_steps: Multiply hours per unit by number of units
misconceptions: Using ≥ or = instead of ≤ for maximum limits.

glossary_entry.definition: Limits resource usage to available amount.

flashcard.front: Write fabricating constraint: Model A 9h, Model B 12h, max 180h.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Fabricating hours: A=9, B=12, max 180. Write constraint.

subject: Mathematics
question: Model A profit = Birr 8,000, Model B profit = Birr 12,000. Fabricating: \(9x+12y \le 180\)
finishing: x+3y \le 30\). What is the maximum profit at vertex (12

explanations_tiered.definition: Profit is linear combination of per-unit profits times quantities.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This vertex is optimal for this problem, giving maximum profit 168,000 Birr.
misconceptions: Adding before multiplying; wrong order of operations.

flashcard.front: Profit A=8000, B=12000. Find profit at (12,6).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Compute profit at (12,6) if A profit 8000, B profit 12000.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Computing total profit at a vertex.

subject: Mathematics
question: A patient needs at least 84 units of drug A and 120 units of drug B. Substance M has 10 units A, 8 units B per gram; substance N has 2 units A, 4 units B per gram. Which constraints represent drug requirements?
explanation: Drug A: \(10x + 2y \ge 84\); Drug B: \(8x+4y \ge 120\). Option A correctly uses ≥ for minimum requirements.

explanations_tiered.definition: Minimum drug requirements are expressed as ≥ constraints.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Total units from M and N must meet or exceed required amounts.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You need at least a certain amount, so total must be greater or equal.
explanations_tiered.advanced: These constraints ensure the patient receives sufficient medication; they are lower-bound constraints.
reasoning_steps: Identify units per gram for each substance

glossary_entry.definition: Lower bound that must be met or exceeded.

flashcard.front: Drug A: M has 10, N has 2. Need ≥84 units. Write constraint.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Drug A: M=10/g, N=2/g, need ≥84. Write constraint.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Both constraints use ≥ for minimum requirements.
